---
title: "Paper 2: The Effect of Compliance Enforcement on Writing Quality: An A/B Experiment"
---

::: {.contribution}
I designed the experimental protocol, secured ethical approval, recruited participants, randomised treatment assignment, collected outcome data, performed the statistical analysis, and wrote the manuscript. Co-author A advised on the experimental design and Co-author B served as a second blind rater.
:::

## Abstract

We report a pre-registered randomised experiment in which 120 doctoral candidates were assigned to write a 5,000-word chapter using either a compliance-enforcing authoring tool (treatment) or an unconstrained alternative (control). Compliance enforcement caused a 0.31 standard-deviation improvement in the blinded quality rating (95% CI: 0.09--0.52, $p = 0.006$).

## Introduction

Paper 1 established that adoption of compliant authoring tools varies systematically across institutions. This paper asks whether adoption *causes* improvements in writing quality through a randomised experiment.

## Methods

### Participants and randomisation

Candidates ($n = 120$) were recruited from three participating universities and randomised at the individual level. Randomisation was stratified by programme year and institution.

### Outcome measure

The primary outcome is a blinded expert rating of overall writing quality on a 1--7 scale, averaged across two independent raters. Inter-rater reliability was high ($\kappa = 0.78$).

## Results

Treatment caused a statistically significant improvement in the quality rating. Mean quality in the treatment group was 4.92 (SD 0.91); mean in control was 4.61 (SD 0.86).

## Discussion

The experimental design provides causal identification of the effect of compliance enforcement on writing quality.

## Limitations

The 5,000-word chapter task may not generalise to the full thesis-writing process.

## Appendix 3.A: Pre-registration

The full pre-registration of hypotheses, analysis plan, and stopping rule is archived at the Open Science Framework.

## Appendix 3.B: Power analysis

The minimum detectable effect was calculated at 0.25 standard deviations at 80% power, $\alpha = 0.05$.

## Appendix 3.C: Robustness checks

The headline result holds across three pre-registered robustness specifications.
